frameworks.name: hyperbolic temporal discounting
frameworks.one_line: Standard discount-rate model that ignores risk attitude in intertemporal choice.

frameworks.name: expected-utility risk preferences
frameworks.one_line: Risk-attitude utility curvature folded into the discounting estimator.

frameworks.name: behavioral economics of intertemporal choice
frameworks.one_line: Joint measurement of time and risk preferences in individual differences.

core_question: Does ignoring individual risk preferences systematically bias estimates of temporal-discounting impulsivity in standard intertemporal choice tasks?
key_assumptions: Delayed rewards are implicitly risky and evaluated under a utility function
Risk attitude and discount rate are separable but jointly identifiable from choice data
Standard hyperbolic discounting omits a utility-curvature term
manipulations: Model specification: hyperbolic discounting vs. risk-augmented discounting
Comparison across individuals differing in risk attitude
key_findings: Standard hyperbolic discounting produces large, systematic bias in estimated discount rates
Apparent impulsivity differences across individuals can be driven by risk-attitude differences instead
Risk-augmented model fits choice data better and de-correlates risk and impulsivity parameters
interpretations: Choice impulsivity measures must jointly estimate risk preferences to be valid for individual-differences research
Reported links between impulsivity and clinical or addictive phenotypes may be confounded by risk attitude
